item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this skip. Full stop. But I would kill to have one, just one interaction log

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you elaborate on that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like the character, but the pacing and tonal issues hold the article back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please someone make a page of this thing talking, or a tale or something. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is unbelievably adorable and one of the few occasions I would want to see an extraneous log demonstrating its antics and/or shenanigans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading the comments, I'm surprised that I seem to be the only one who thought this would be one hell of a cover. While unlikely, perhaps the spybot is exactly where it wants to be, and says exactly what it means to. If they didn't have some insight into it's thought processes and/or it didn't try to violently escape, the Foundation would be far more wary. This way, it seems sad and eccentric and only a little dangerous, and they keep it out and being tested. I'm assuming that the only reason they "know" that its knowledge scans require direct skin contact is that it has said so.
+1, BTW. Honestly I like it better as intended. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my God, this is absolutely and totally brilliant. I can't comment on previous revisions, because I won't read them for fear of spoiling this magnificence. I was all set to downvote for a circular thing, but this is done so damned well. I love broken things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. You made http://xkcd.com/695/ into an SCP.

item.title: Re: Typo fixed.
item.content:encoded: Next time, please note what you changed in the comment of the edit itself since that shows up on Recent Changes.

item.title: Typo fixed.
item.content:encoded: I fixed a minor typo. "in particular, it is does not know who it is supposed to spy on, or on whose behalf." became "in particular, it does not know who it is supposed to spy on, or on whose behalf."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: THIS. DO THIS. DO IT. DO IT DO IT DO IT>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-507 didn't ask to be called "Grabnok the Destroyer": it's one of a list of nicknames given to him by other people.

He refused to give his actual name, and someone thought Grabnok was a cool nickname to give him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Voct I couldn't decide if I should laugh or cry XD. Please add that conversation as an interview log….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ IRC discussion
- well, /I/ thought it was interesting

<Voct> "And what else can you do with your … legs?"
<Voct> "I CAN SHUFFLE CARDS I CAN TIE SHOES ASK ME IF THERE'S ANYTHING ELSE ASK ME ABOUT USING A GAROTTE"
<Dauss> Hahaha
<Voct> "…. is there anything… else?"
<Voct> "YES THERE IS. OH PLEASE PLEASE ASK ME IF I CAN USE A GAROTTE PLEASE OH PLEASE ASK ME TO DEMONSTRATE"
<Dauss> ROLL CIGARETTES OPEN A DOOR LOAD A GUN DO YOU WANT TO SEE ME LOAD A GUN
<Voct> "Since you're an espionage tool, can you… use weapons? For instance, a garotte?"
<Voct> "YES OH YES HE SAID IT OH SO LUCKY YES I CAN USE A GAROTTE ASK ME TO DEMONSTRATE YOU FUCKER ASK ME TO DEMONSTRATE WOULD YOU LIKE A DEMONSTRATION"
<Voct> "No thank you, that won't be necessary."
<Dauss> 'FFFFFFFFFUUUUUUUUU'
<Voct> "Are you… disappointed?"
<Dauss> 'No. YES YES YES'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for delayed response. I just checked this out again, and the revisions are an enormous improvement—particularly in terms of my prior criticism.

My -1 has become a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's one of the alternatives I considered, or a disembodied consciousness somehow stored in this device. Either way, it's clearly highly disciplined and skilled, likely pretty intelligent, could presumably have spy training, and might just have been specifically created for this purpose. So many possibilities means a lot of bases to cover.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could be a brain in a box (with some trauma induced tics and damage) but examination always turns up solid ceramics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't read the old version; I suppose the author just figured this was a enough of a downgrade threat-wise that it merited a change in security level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
True, and I suppose at that point an upgrade would probably more seriously considered. Still, I wouldn't want to rule out the chance that this thing is smart enough to not consciously think about the revelation once it starts putting the pieces together; perhaps it would quickly write a new subroutine into its programming, so it would be subconsciously aware and able to disguise the fact without conscious thought. Again, no clue how advanced the AI is, but I could see this being the solution to some cheep sci-fi robot's plight, so I wouldn't want to rule it out. Perhaps I'm grasping at straws, but just the fact that something plausibly could happen if the Foundation doesn't actively watch this thing seems like enough to merit a Euclid rating.

Edit: Just realized that there's actually no reason to completely assume this is an AI, but it just strikes me as the most likely explanation. Rule out that part about subroutines if you disagree, but I'm sure a highly disciplined and creative mind (for example, a spy) could achieve the same effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would actually blurt out the revelation that the doctors can hear it think…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know about the Faraday cage, but again, this thing is likely pretty smart, and if/when it figures out we can hear it, say it decided to play the "sleep" message early, get's taken out for study, and picks up a signal? One of at least a few plausible scenarios that I can think of, and again, do you really want to rule this kind of thing out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was euclid before the rewrite… not sure why it was downgraded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It can't pick up the radio frequency it's looking for, because we're keeping it in a Faraday cage. No signals can penetrate a Faraday cage.

As for the "false sense of security" - that's what the comment at the end is about, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Idk if it's been brought up already, but shouldn't this thing be classified as Euclid? Sure it can't remember what its purpose is and therefore poses no identifiable threat to the Foundation at this time, but it clearly actively engages in escape attempts, has demonstrated the ability to kill Foundation personal, and is likely sentient. Sure the Foundation can hear its thoughts, but this thing is obviously not completely stupid. After a while it would probably pick up that the Foundation can hear its thoughts from the way researchers act around it (I don't care how much procedure you have during tests, it only takes one minor slip-up). Or what if its already figured out the Foundation can hear its thoughts and is lulling them into into a false sense of security? Or what if it just decides to act on impulse when an escape opportunity presents itself, gets lucky, and succeeds? Plus, nobody knows what will happen if this thing manages to somehow pick up the radio frequency it's searching for, and in this business I find it's usually better to be safe than sorry.

Edit: Spelling/grammar fixes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noted and upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Point taken; fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removing my downvote as this is radically different than its original incarnations and is now not bad. However, I have one nitpick before I upvote. Does an O5 really need to be the one authoring the note at the end? Can't it be the Site Director? I feel like the Overseers wouldn't get directly involved in the administration of Safe class objects, and as a literary device, I feel like they've been overused of late. When O5 shows up in a report, it should evoke a "DAMN SHIT GOT REAL" feeling, which I don't necessarily think is called for here. This guy is more a subtle menace, and better for it.

item.title: Re: AHA
item.content:encoded:
It was spying on [DAMAGED SECTOR ON DISK], on behalf of the [FILE BAD OR MISSING].

Interesting. It's actually vaguely similar to the backstory I came up with for a character I created for the game Champions Online - Happifun Security System X-4 was undergoing testing when the Qularr attack overloaded its systems, and it emerged from the rubble of Happifun Robotics with damaged files. It knew it was designed to secure a given area and defeat designated foes, but couldn't remember the specifics. Fortunately, the first person it encountered was a Millennium City police officer, who gave it mission parameters (without meaning to)…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting the second rewrite, because now you've got a more interesting object here. Like I said to Voct earlier, though, I like that it's damaged but wish that the damage didn't take away its subtlety. Sure it's a lot easier to figure out its backstory if its speech circuits are overactive, but the only question this leaves then is "where did it come from?" which isn't as interesting to me as "what" or "why". Still, upvoting for the improved description (and the mental image of a ceramic disc successfully using a garotte).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon emerging from its dormancy, SCP-1599 vocalizes an entry in its internal systems log; this entry counts the number of dormancy cycles since SCP-1599 entered Foundation custody, affirms that SCP-1599 is not worthless and that its superiors have neither abandoned it nor written it off as destroyed, and documents the status of its constant search for a broadcast signal by which it can recalibrate its damaged programming.

DORMANCY CYCLE CONCLUDED. NUMBER TWO … ONE … SIX SINCE CAPTURE.

REPAIR UNITS WILL BE ATTRACTED TO MY POSITIVE ENERGY. I HAVE MORE THAN ENOUGH POWER REMAINING TO COMPLETE MY MISSION. I FORGIVE MYSELF FOR THE MALFUNCTION THAT LED TO MY CAPTURE. I AM WORTHY OF A PROLONGED SEARCH BY █████████████ ████████. IF FEELINGS OF UNWORTHINESS ARISE, I GO DEEPER AND RECOGNIZE THEM AS ILLUSIONS. I BOLDLY FACE AND OVERCOME NEW CHALLENGES TO MY MISSION.

MISSION: TO [brief burst of static].
[five-second pause]
CORRUPTED DATA IN MISSION OBJECTIVES. RECALIBRATION NEEDED.

SEARCHING FOR RECALIBRATION SIGNAL ON FREQUENCY NINE … ONE … SIX … FIVE. SIGNAL NOT FOUND.
SEARCHING FOR RECALIBRATION SIGNAL ON FREQUENCY NINE … ONE … SIX … SIX. SIGNAL NOT FOUND.
SEARCHING FOR RECALIBRATION SIGNAL ON FREQUENCY NINE … ONE … SIX … SEVEN. SIGNAL NOT FOUND.
SEARCHING FOR RECALIBRATION SIGNAL ON FREQUENCY NINE … ONE … SIX … EIGHT. SIGNAL NOT FOUND.
SEARCHING FOR RECALIBRATION SIGNAL ON FREQUENCY NINE … ONE … SIX … NINE. SIGNAL NOT FOUND.

[sniffle]

I'm fine, I'm fine. Allergies. And I got something in my eye.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haven't read any of the previous versions. Like the current one a lot. Upvoted.

EDIT: Just read the newly added end note. Now like it even more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an onomatopoeia of the Viper(probe) Droid's transmissions from The Empire Strikes Back. That's my first impression of this SCP idea, from the appearance to what it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wizard of Id.

Another thing. Another thing.

Okay, the thing is, this will live or die on its backstory and interactions. And as a spy, it should be concealing stuff, and how can we know all this. Why would it tell us all this.

Answer: it can't help telling us this stuff. Its subvocalization routine is broken.

It says everything it's thinking, out loud. All the time.

When it plans to murder a researcher, we know it, because it says "when the researcher turns his head to the left, I will jump at his throat and slice through his carotid. I must act nonchalant. I will project a hologram of an attractive woman to distract him."

And it doesn't realize that we can hear it.

Every subjective morning, when it wakes up, it makes an entry in its internal log… out loud.

That's how we know it's a spy, that's how we know it's forgotten who it's spying on or for whom, that's how we know it's looking for its calibration signal.

We'd love to know more about how it works, but it doesn't have access to that data.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is that from? Red Dwarf?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Frimmin' on the frim fram. Frimmin on the fram.

item.title: Re: AHA
item.content:encoded: I like where you're going with this.

item.title: Re: AHA
item.content:encoded: It must have a reeeeeally efficient battery if it is solar powered…

item.title: Re: AHA
item.content:encoded:
It speaks thirty languages. It can change color (but always goes back to black when it switches into sleep mode). It's solar-powered. It claims that it can hover, although it's never done this since being taken into custody (is the hover utility damaged, or its little artificial mind broken - we don't know).

It can pick locks, and tie and untie knots, and roll cigarettes (we have no idea why it has this set of skills). It says it can use a garotte, and load a gun, but we've decided that we don't need to see that.

item.title: AHA
item.content:encoded:
Figured it out.

It's a spy. It's an espionage bot. It was spying on [DAMAGED SECTOR ON DISK], on behalf of the [FILE BAD OR MISSING]. It's so badly damaged (not just the broken legs and the mismade holograms) that it doesn't know what its exact purpose is.

It's sure that if it escapes, it will be able to pick up a broadcast signal from its supervisors and recalibrate itself and remember what its mission is (thus the Faraday cage).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I'm basing that on your original concept.

I've got some notions…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not entirely sure where Voct's rewrite is going. He keeps trying to limit it to attractive humanoid holograms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anything anomalous can be an SCP, but to be a good SCP, it needs a point and a hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a broken machine with sapience… what more point does it need to be an SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So what's the point?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So did you like it better when it was a crazy broken thing that actually attempted to drive a researcher insane?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a serious improvement over the first draft. However, I just can't get behind your execution.

The first 3 paragraphs of the description and the addendum all read like you were trying to make this funny or cute. You cram the only semi-creepy info into the 4th paragraph of the description, and it's understated to the point where it feels tacked on.

For example, it convinces people to let it escape? Ok. If you provided an example of the thing actually being manipulative, devious, or otherwise sinister, I might be able to get behind it. Right now, it seems like just a friendly pathetic robot companion that makes goofy requests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My firm belief is that the researchers in question would use the scp's name as a matter of course since they see it every day, and are only human…but that that name does not need to be mentioned in the official documentation unless it is the Object Name as shown in the main list. But then again I have a warmer foundation than some headcanons…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In regards to Larry the Loving Llama, I think that falls under the "in some circumstances" clause as long as it's kept just to conversations WITH Larry. Again, favouring easy communication over strict adherence to naming convention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: sure just call 1-800-dreadlord

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anybody remember the Foundation Dreadlord hotline number? I think THE SCREAMING MAN is loose again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ^This. They wouldn't bother with it except that it helps better facilitate communication with an SCP where communication is already a difficult issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've read it over about five times, and after that and the conversation in chat, I can't deny that Voct's rewrite really is good and has genuinely piqued my interest.
However, I think this article falls short in that, even though I really enjoy being left wondering what the disc wants after it manages to escape confinement, I want more. I personally need some sort of stimulation as to the benign/malignant/neutral nature of this thing and its overall desires. An interview log of some sort, I think, would really round it out and complete it.
It's definitely not material for a vote down.
Pending any further development, I keep an up-vote in reserve for it. I really love this one, just not enough to give affirmative support as it is.

<edit: fixed incomplete thought>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not bad, but it's not really interesting either. The entire second paragraph sounds weird. -1 for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea why this would be a problem. I find the prospect of this somehow setting a precedent where every SCP wants to be named after an existing deity hilarious, but unlikely. I also don't understand why this behaviour would be considered unacceptable. It's not like we're going to piss off the Sumerians if we humor it along.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not that it's used somewhere else, it is that she is wanting to be named after a deity, would you say the Foundation would want to enforce that behaviour?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really care if it's used somewhere else? No one objects to calling 073 "Cain", though he may be distinct from the biblical entity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems very stilted. Every sentence is right to a point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But it's time for your Colonoscopy.
